summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Adjournment of final hearing – Where the parties to the proceedings were the husband, wife, and the husband’s father as a second respondent – Where the second respondent had been joined as a consequence of his and the husband’s assertion that he had a right to occupy a property – Where the husband’s case changed such that he advised the Court the second respondent was not asserting any right or interest in any property of the parties that is the subject of these proceedings – Where the wife’s final relief seeks an immediate sale of the property – Where if the Court finds that the second respondent has a right to occupy, the relief the wife seeks cannot be made – Where the second respondent is an 86-year-old man who is not legally qualified and is self-represented – Where the second respondent has consistently maintained ever since his joinder as a party to the proceedings that he has a right to occupy the property – Where a party must be given notice of and sufficient opportunity to be heard where an order is sought that would deprive them of a right or interest – Where the hearing could not proceed and the Court vacated the final hearing dates.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

On 27 January 2026, the Court vacated the final hearing dates and indicated that it would in due course deliver reasons for the adjournment.
These are those reasons.

BACKGROUND

On 27 June 2025, the Court listed the outstanding Initiating Application and Response for a final hearing to commence on 2 February 2026. At that stage, the only issues for determination before the Court were proceedings pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The parties to the proceedings were the husband, wife, and the husband’s father as a second respondent. The second respondent had been joined by order made 11 June 2025 as a consequence of his and the husband’s assertion that he had a right to occupy a property at Suburb H (the “[Suburb H] property”).
The orders made that day provided for the parties to file certain documents including a Balance Sheet, a List of Objections, a Case Outline, a Joint Chronology, a List of Issues and a List of Factual Findings.
The hearing dates were later varied to commence a week earlier on 27 January 2026.
On 10 November 2025, the wife filed an Amended Response seeking parenting orders which sought to vary what had been the established regime of equal shared care to one where the children lived primarily with her and spent time with the husband each alternate weekend. In that Amended Response, the wife also sought an order pursuant to s 106B of the Act setting aside a transfer of shares in a company, J Pty Ltd, that had allegedly occurred in February 2025.
On 16 October 2025, the second respondent’s solicitor filed a Notice of Discontinuance.
The parties’ affidavits were due to be filed by no later than 7 November 2025. No party complied with that direction. The wife’s affidavit was filed on 10 November 2025. The husband filed his affidavit on 26 November 2025 and the second respondent, through the solicitor who had previously acted for him, filed on 26 November 2025.
The husband in his affidavit contended that a property at Suburb H registered in the husband’s sole name was held subject to the second respondent’s right of occupancy. In that respect, his evidence on that issue was as follows:
My father, [Mr B Fry] and his step-mother my step-grandmother is [Mrs K].
In [1964], [Mr B Fry’s] father purchased [L Street] [Suburb H] (the “[Suburb H] property”) and placed the property in [Mrs K’s] sole name.
...
In [2006], [Mrs K] passed away. At that time, I understood [Mrs K’s] Last Will and Testament provided for the [Suburb H] property to be transferred into my name, however it was subject to a grant of a Life Estate for [Mr B Fry] to live in the [Suburb H] property. My father is the Executor of [Mrs K’s] Will.
In [2007], a Grant of Probate was made through the Supreme Court of New South Wales.
On the granting of Probate, the property was not transferred into my personal name.
At or about this time, my father and I had a conversation to the effect of:
[Mr B Fry]: “It makes sense if you stay living in the [Suburb H] property and I will come and go from it. I will continue to pay you all of the outgoings associated with the property and each December I will review whether I want to move back there permanently.”

Me: “Ok Dad, that is fine with me.”
From that time, [Mr B Fry] has paid all of the outgoings in connection with the [Suburb H] property. [Mr B Fry] has either paid the bills directly or provided me with money to pay the bills as part of the loan he has provided.
...
In [2009], my father [Mr B Fry] and I entered into a Loan Agreement so that I could borrow money off him. I had a conversation with my father to the effect of:
I said: “Dad, I want to get the business going and I will need access to money. Are you in a position to lend me some money which I will repay?”

[Mr B Fry] said: “Yes, I am, but we will need to document it.”

I said: “Ok, we will get a loan agreement prepared.”
...

78. At about this time, I had a conversation with my father to the effect of:
I said: “Dad, I am trying to get some loans so that I can get some work off the ground. Borrowing at the moment is hard as I do not have any collateral to secure the borrowings against. If you would agree to transfer to me [Suburb H] now, I will be able to borrow money which will help [Ms N] and I out majorly.”

Dad said: “[Mr Fry] if I transfer the property to you, I need to have the ability to move back to the home when I get older. This is the home in which I intend to live in for my latter part of my life as it is of considerable sentimental value to me and will accommodate me in a way that I need to be looked after and have my needs met when I am less able.”

I said: “That is fine Dad, this house will always be here for you to have and to use for the duration of your life. I will honour that agreement as I know that your Dad and [Mrs K] promised you this.”
I obtained a loan from [M Company] for the purchase of land for $1,200,000 and then subsequently consolidated all the loans into [O Bank]. This was only possible using [Suburb H] as collateral.
In [2015], my father transferred to me the [Suburb H] property...

...
My main goal is to maintained continued ownership of the [Suburb H] property so that my father can stay in this property until he passes away.
I ow [sic] my father the commitment I made to him which is consistent with the Life Estate he has in the property and I ask the Court to make an Order for me to retain the [Suburb H] property on a final basis.
If the [Suburb H] property is sold, I will not be able to provide to my father his continued use and access to the property which I had agreed to him I would, necessary for him to transfer the property to me so that I could encumber it for the [Suburb P] investment.

(As per the original, annexures omitted)
The second respondent’s affidavit largely accords with the evidence of the husband. His evidence goes on to say the following:
In [June 2025], I telephoned [Mr Fry] and told him I intended to move back into the [Suburb H] property immediately. We discussed [Mr Fry] and the children would remain living in the property with [Ms Q] and I. This will allow [Mr Fry] to assist [Ms Q] and I into the future with our medical issues.
Moving back into the [Suburb H] property accords with my father's wishes to allow me to live out my life interest in the property.
I say that I have always had a right to live in that property until my death and that right was not extinguished by the conveyance of title to [Mr Fry] in 2015.
Even if I am wrong about this, I conveyed title of the property to [Mr Fry] on an expressed promise that I would continue to have a right to live in the property until my death. [Mr Fry] holds the property on trust on my behalf until such time that I pass away.
I ask the Court to make declarations and orders in accordance with the Application in a Proceeding which is filed herewith.
On 3 November 2025, orders were made that for the parties to file Undertakings as to Disclosure by 4.00 pm on 5 December 2025. Neither party complied with the order. The husband filed an Undertaking as to Disclosure on 23 January 2026; the wife has not complied with the order despite complaining at length of a failure on the part of the husband to comply with his disclosure obligations.
Despite what was said by the husband in his affidavit filed 26 November 2025, his case changed between that date and 17 December 2025. In that respect, on 17 December 2025, the matter was listed for a compliance check hearing. The Court made notations on that day which included that the husband was not asserting that the second respondent had any “right or interest in any property of the parties that is the subject of these proceedings” (Notation C). At that appearance, the representation in Notation C was made by the husband. There was no appearance by the second respondent.
No party complied with Orders 10, 14, 15, 16, 17 and 18 made 27 June 2025 in relation to the filing of documents for the hearing.
The husband’s Outline of Case document identified the second respondent as a witness in his case.
The wife’s Outline of Case document included orders seeking a sale of the Suburb H property.
At the commencement of the hearing, the husband (despite his sworn evidence) advised the Court that it was his case that his father had no interest in the Suburb H property. The apparent inconsistency in the husband’s case on the issue of the second respondent’s right to occupy on one view seemed to have eluded the husband.
As the second respondent was a witness in the husband’s case, he was not present in court at the commencement of the hearing. The second respondent was called into court and was asked what his case was as to the right to occupy. He advised the Court, consistent with his affidavit and contrary to the husband’s representation on 17 December 2025, that he had a right of occupation in the Suburb H property.
The second respondent is an 86-year-old man who is not legally qualified and is self-represented. He has consistently maintained, ever since his joinder as a party to the proceedings, that he has a right to occupy the Suburb H property. The interests of the husband and the second respondent conflict and it is entirely inappropriate that he appears merely as a witness in the husband’s case.
Counsel for the wife contended that the second respondent’s right to occupy the property which arose as a consequence of a provision in the will carried with it an obligation to make various payments. The wife’s counsel contended that because the wife had made some of the payments required to be made by the second respondent, his right to occupy had been extinguished. The husband and the second respondent both say that he has made the payments as required by the terms of the will. Whether the payments have been made is a factual issue at the final hearing. There exists a further issue in relation to the right to occupy and that is the agreement made in June 2009 and the representations made by the husband which on one view of the evidence, the second respondent may have acted to his detriment.
The wife’s final relief seeks an immediate sale of the Suburb H property. In the event the Court finds that the second respondent has a right to occupy, whether arising under the will or the oral agreement, the relief the wife seeks cannot be made. In those circumstances, it may be that the proper course is an adjournment of the proceedings pursuant to s 79AA(5) of the Act.
Part of the wife’s final relief also included orders pursuant to s 106B of the Act setting aside the appointment of the second respondent as a director of C Pty Ltd and for the wife to be appointed a trustee to sell C Pty Ltd Entity 2 and C Pty Ltd Entity 3. The wife’s counsel conceded that no notice had been given to the second respondent or the companies of the orders sought.
It is a fundamental tenant of procedural fairness that a party is given notice of and sufficient opportunity to be heard where an order is sought that would deprive them of a right or interest (Kioa v West (1985) 159 CLR 550). The hearing could not proceed.

DISPOSITION

These are the reasons for why the hearing dates were vacated.
